display_title: 1987-03_04 — ORIENTEERING IN THE LAND OF THE RISING SUN
body_html:
Page 1

Bearing 315
MAR-APR 1987
the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club

ORIENTEERING IN THE LAND OF THE RISING SUN

Eileen Williams

[Japanese Calligraphy sidebar]

The challenge begins long before the race ever starts... when you receive the quarterly schedule and then track down a Japanese friend to decipher the tiny Kanji characters as to where the event is to be held. After which you troupe down to the local post office to pay your registration fee into a bank-like account specially set up for each particular meet. After being passed from one confused, non-comprehending postal worker to another, you pay your entry fee and receive a two-part postcard to mail your application to the event organizer. They will send the second part back to you with your race start time. All this takes place as much as two months in advance for the bigger meets. For those meets, you receive a printed booklet with your start time and category, which stands out in English letters among the thousands of Kanji-lettered Japanese names.

Your next challenge is to find the right train to the meet site. If your decipherer was accurate, you will arrive on time at the correct station to be met by a host club orienteer who directs you, along with many others, to the specially scheduled bus. The bus winds along into the countryside. You begin your mental preparations for the day as the all-Japanese bus fills with the sound of orienteering chatter, no doubt the usual kind of map talk, but you understand very little of it.

Arriving at the end of the line, the bus empties and you follow the crowd 5-10 minutes down the road to the changing area. Sometimes this is multiple tents in the woods, other times a large gymnasium, but always you look for your club banner. In this case, it's Kohnan Club of Yokohama, who have adopted you from the first meet you attended. You remember to take off your shoes at the door in traditional Japanese fashion. After exchanging greetings, stretching out, and studying the map cutout taped to the wall, you are ready for the 20-30 minute jog to the start. With your clubmates, you follow the white flagging.

Arriving at the start, you can see organization at it's best here, with as many as 10 courses being run simultaneously, without a hitch. Into...

Page 2

the starting gate you go, as they call out your appointed time three minutes before the actual time. The beep of the clock signals, and you are off and running!

In the "A" level courses, description sheets are written in International Symbols, but for easier level courses, map reading is your only clue as to what feature you are searching for. Kanji characters are not only on the description sheet, but in the map legend as well. "Gambatte!" as they say, meaning "Good luck." Luckily, map reading is your forte, and the Japanese make detailed and accurate maps.

Working your way around the crest of a hill, across a path, and back into the thick on the other side, you are amazed at the crowds of people in the woods around you, swarming every which way. And every age level, from children to sturdy old folks, are there. Trying not to be distracted, you crash on through the first half of your course. Ah, an easy leg next: down from the wooded hillside, across the ditch, now work your way around the rice fields, careful not to upset the farmer's crops. Oh! Watch out for cars as you cross the road - but there's no danger here, because the meet organizers have stationed a lookout person to make sure cars don't interrupt the race. Reaching the far side, you navigate between houses and orchards to begin the climb up through densely-wooded bamboo groves. You curse your chosen route, but hope it gains some time. Almost to the control now, but oh! take care not to step into the little Buddhist shrine cemetery. It is one of many which dot the hillsides, symbolized on your map by little black crosses. Finally, you reach the control in the depression, punch in, and are on your way. Before you reach the finish, you will have passed through a variety of interesting terrain, from construction sites to farm-terraced hillsides, to deep and mysterious bamboo forests.

The finish scene is familiar the world over, with small groups gathering over their maps to talk about the day's courses and route choices. What is perhaps unique to Japan are the picnics and sharing of food, which make this a well-rounded social event, while waiting for the prompt posting of times and awards announcements. The train ride home will be several hours long, so the hot soup offered by the organizers, along with the sushi and oranges you've brought, revive your energies.

Waving "Sayonara" to your orienteering friends, you return to the city, refreshed with the day's memories of the natural beauty that Japan's people and countryside have to offer.

Mo ichi do orienteering o suru tsumori desu!

(My interpreter says this means "we will go orienteering again" - Ed.)

Page 3

FAMILY PLAN

We are introducing a new fee category at our meets. From now on, families who have family memberships in the club will pay full fee only for the first two members. Additional family members will pay only $1.00 per registration. We hope this will encourage more families to participate in orienteering. This special fee does not apply to organizations (such as Scouts) with family memberships.

CLUB ELECTIONS REVISITED

At our last general meeting, Mike Schuh (nominated from the floor) was elected President of COC, but Ardis Dull, who was going to run for Secretary, withdrew because of illness. So, we now have two elected officers, Mike Schuh and Doug Sprugel, who was elected Treasurer last November. We still need a Vice President and a Secretary. On March 9 at the Green Lake Library (7364 E. Green Lake Dr. N.), we will try again to elect a full slate of officers.

The elections will be preceded by a showing of the videotape "Orienteering - A Sense of Direction," which is about the 1985 World O' Championships in Australia.

TREASURER'S REPORT - 1986

Doug Sprugel

In brief, our main sources of ordinary (non-Trollhaugen) income are memberships ($809 total) and meet registrations ($983.60: don't ask me where the 60 cents came from). Membership income roughly covers the cost of printing the newsletter, USOF fees, publicity, and administration, while meet fees and map sales nearly cover the cost of putting on meets plus the cost of making and printing maps to hold future meets on. The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League is pretty much self-sustaining; we accidentally made a profit this year, but it isn't planned that way.

The Trollhaugen meet was by far our biggest budget item, both in income and outgo. Based on 1986 figures alone, Trollhaugen almost exactly broke even. This is a little misleading because we spent about $850 in 1985 on the early stages of the mapmaking process, but on the other hand we have about 700 copies of the map left, with a street value of about $875 ($1.25 each). In addition, the equipment bought for the meet (for example, the timing clock) will be useful for many years. In fact, it's debatable whether the equipment should even be considered a Trollhaugen expense. The most important benefits of the Big Meet, of course, are intangible: we have an exciting new place to hold meets (which we will use at least twice this year), generated favorable publicity for Northwestern orienteering, and I for one had a lot of fun working on and running in a major meet without having to fly halfway across the country.

CASCADE CHATTER

Harvey "Naturally Inquisitive" Friedman

Robert and Sandy Miller's new car (which I told you about last issue) was parked in front of their house when a collision involving two other vehicles threw one of the vehicles against their new car with such force that it couldn't be driven. It has been over two weeks now that they haven't had use of their new toy. Let's hope the body shop makes it as good as new and quickly... For those of you who want to contact Don Denbo, he is at 220 Central St., Stoneham, MA 02180... Club founder Len Englund was discussing having an anniversary celebration of some kind to commemorate COC being in existence for 10 years. Anyone interested in a banquet? Contact Len or a board member with any suggestions or good ideas on it... Anyone who wants to write to Eileen Williams in Japan can contact her at 3-22-8 Jujonakahara, Kita-Ku, Tokyo 114 JAPAN. Use air mail, because surface mail took over two months. Anyone who wants to read her first card, just ask me... We have six new memberships in the club. We need more, so everyone try to enlist a new member or so... If more people tell me what is going on, I will be able to write it up in this column.

LOCAL FOLKS MAKE GOOD

Several local residents have been recognized by U.S. Orienteering Team coach Peter Gagarin for their performances in "A" meets in 1986. Heading the list is Nathan Tharp (Nisqually Orienteers), who was one of nine people named to the U.S. Junior Men's A Team. Mary Forbes (Nisqually) was named to the Senior Women's B Team, and Ron Pontius (Nisqually and Blue Star Komplex) was named to the Senior Men's B Team. Named to the "Bullpen" (people who have done well) were Sammamish O.C.'s Dave Enger in M21A, and Cascade's own Debbie Newell and Eileen Williams in F21A, and Jessica Hirschfelder in the Junior Women's division. Debbie was also named "rookie of the year" in class F21A. Congratulations to all these people, and keep up the good work.

MESSAGE FROM THE PREZ

Mike Schuh

One of the many things I want to do as club President this year is to improve communications within the club, especially between the club's executive board and the general membership. One part of this will be this column; others will include regular club meetings and "Pot-0'-Luck" dinners, club picnics, and social get-togethers after our meets.

We will have a club meeting at 6:30 p.m., on March 9 at the Green Lake Library (7464 E. Green Lake Dr. N.). The meeting will start with the showing of the video tape "Orienteering - A Sense of Direction", which features the 1985 World Orienteering Championships in Australia. Afterwards we will have a...

Page 6

brief business meeting, including the election of club Secretary (and perhaps club Vice President). I will also entertain a BRIEF discussion of club goals and members' concerns.

Plans for the May meeting include a talk on training tips and ideas on how to improve your navigational skills. The July meeting will feature a report from this year's USOF convention and Western U.S. Orienteering Championships, both of which take place in San Diego in June.

The first of this year's Pot-O'-Lucks will be held April 4th; see the details elsewhere in this issue. Judy Cohen, the club's "Social Director," is responsible for organizing these (one will be held every other month), and the other social activities. Call her if you'd like to host one of the Pot-O'-Lucks.

1987 marks the tenth anniversary of Cascade Orienteering Club as an organized club; we will be celebrating this occasion with appropriate festivities this fall. More on this as plans develop - if you'd like to help, give me a call. And next year, in July, we plan to host the 1988 Western U.S. Orienteering Championships. Exciting things are happening - join in the fun and help make them even more exciting!

LETTERS

Dear Editor,

Knut (Ka-noot) Olsen, 62 year old U.S. champion and Swede extraordinaire, has accepted the publicity coordinator position. I, ex-publicity man, would have liked to be the first to congratulate him, unfortunately that opportunity vanished like a valuable object stolen in the night (the thief, I believe, was the misplacement of "objectives," whether personal or business, before "people"). I was far from first, in seemingly clandestine changes, to learn that someone's dissatisfaction or striving for improvements has, rather than merely expressing itself, taken action. I feel like I just met Rambo.

Such changes should not be so sudden. The Club should maybe not copy the world that the news media brings us: Iranscam, Ivan Boesky in Wall Street, etc.

What I have learned is that doing long stints of volunteer work it is easy to accumulate expectations of return other than enjoying the sport itself. I've learned that the appreciation was there, but like a hard-to-find control feature, I sometimes expected it to look different. Sometimes I asked for it, subtly, like I was asking for money.

PUBLICITY is a big word. It is like saying "I work at Boeing." What Knut does will be truly Knut-Publicity. I will pass along to him tips and tats of info and leads that many, many of you club members have passed to me. (Thanks). Anyone with ideas could help Knut by passing along a name and number or a publication address with your tip.

Page 7

I would like to thank those who responded to newsletter notices and helped me out. I hope you do the same for Knut, his accent is the only complex thing about Him!

Roger Sharp

WIOL CHAMPIONSHIPS

Seward Park, February 14

Pleasant weather greeted the more than 80 orienteers that attended the Fifth Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Championships February 14 in Seattle's Seward Park. In addition to the league course, there were four other courses available, ranging from 2 km to over 5 km.

The WIOL course saw some good competition, with many places being decided by just a few seconds. Jeff Coker, from Wilson, was the men's winner, and Pam Kosacki, from Yelm, was the women's winner. In a rare occurence, Nathan Tharp, from Timberline, mispunched near the end of the course, and was disqualified. Tacoma's Wilson High School, with Carl Moore coaching, won the team trophy again.

On the longest course, Dale Width showed that the residents of the wet side of the mountains do not have a monopoly on orienteering prowess. Likewise, Arne Skog and Dave Lilly demonstrated that Sammamish OC has a few good orienteers as well. It is interesting to note that Robert Miller, just 37 seconds slower than Dave, walked/jogged the course due to injuries. Sometimes accuracy can be just as useful as speed...

Debbie Newell and Sandy Kish Miller lead the way on course 3, showing what a bit of experience can do.

A couple of people forgot to punch the last control: Fred Veler, on Course 4, and Don Atkinson on Course 3. Otherwise, they would have had fast times.

After the event, there was a picnic to benefit the school league. This was well received and many commented that they would like to see such offerings become a standard part of orienteering meets in the future.

Thanks go to Kurt Blakstad for design of the novice course and helping set up and take down the finish area; Gary McCausland for help with WIOL registration, setting up, and making the school league awards; Pat Albright for marking the WIOL maps and registration and providing transportation; Dick Diesner for manning the start; Dave Enger for vetting the WIOL course and working at the finish; and Peggy Tharp for organizing the picnic. Due to the large turnout, several club members stepped in without being asked and helped out. These included Debbie Newell and Harvey Friedman, who worked at registration; Arnold Kay, who instructed beginners and then worked with Dave at the finish for the rest of the day; Peggy Tharp, who greeted newcomers; and Carl Moore and Mike Tharp, who assisted at the...

WANTED: LOGO

At a meeting held on February 7th, a new orienteering organization was born: the Washington State Orienteering Association. Loosely composed of a group of interested orienteers in Washington, the purpose of the association is to help clubs promote and develop orienteering on a statewide basis.

One of the group's priorities is to have a logo (you can't have stationery without a logo), so they are sponsoring a logo design contest. There won't be any prizes (like a round trip ticket to Sweden), but it should be fun.

If you are interested in submitting a logo design, please bring it to the next meeting of the WSOA, which will be in Cle Elum on May 23rd, right after the Kittitas Valley Orienteering Festival (see schedule).

POT-O-LUCK

COC's first pot-O-luck of 1987 will be held on April 4th at the home of Judy Cohen, beginning at 6:30 p.m. Bring your favorite dish and see what everyone looks like without mud all over them.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 MAR-APR 1987 the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
ORIENTEERING IN THE LAND OF THE RISING SUN

The challenge begins long before the race ever starts... when you receive the quarterly schedule and then track down a Japanese friend to decipher the tiny Kanji characters as to where the event is to be held. After which you troupe down to the local post office to pay your registration fee into a bank-like account specially set up for each particular meet. After being passed from one confused, non-comprehending postal worker to another, you pay your entry fee and receive a two-part postcard to mail your application to the event organizer. They will send the second part back to you with your race start time. All this takes place as much as two months in advance for the bigger meets. For those meets, you receive a printed booklet with your start time and category, which stands out in English letters among the thousands of Kanji-lettered Japanese names.

Your next challenge is to find the right train to the meet site. If your decipherer was accurate, you will arrive on time at the correct station to be met by a host club orienteer who directs you, along with many others, to the specially scheduled bus. The bus winds along into the countryside. You begin your mental preparations for the day as the all-Japanese bus fills with the sound of orienteering chatter, no doubt the usual kind of map talk, but you understand very little of it.

Arriving at the end of the line, the bus empties and you follow the crowd 5-10 minutes down the road to the changing area. Sometimes this is multiple tents in the woods, other times a large gymnasium, but always you look for your club banner. In this case, it's Kohnan Club of Yokohama, who have adopted you from the first meet you attended. You remember to take off your shoes at the door in traditional Japanese fashion. After exchanging greetings, stretching out, and studying the map cutout taped to the wall, you are ready for the 20-30 minute jog to the start. With your clubmates, you follow the white flagging.
Arriving at the start, you can see organization at it's best here, with as many as 10 courses being run simultaneously, without a hitch. Into...
the starting gate you go, as they call out your appointed time three minutes before the actual time. The beep of the clock signals, and you are off and running!

In the "A" level courses, description sheets are written in International Symbols, but for easier level courses, map reading is your only clue as to what feature you are searching for. Kanji characters are not only on the description sheet, but in the map legend as well. "Gambatte!" as they say, meaning "Good luck." Luckily, map reading is your forte, and the Japanese make detailed and accurate maps.

Working your way around the crest of a hill, across a path, and back into the thick on the other side, you are amazed at the crowds of people in the woods around you, swarming every which way. And every age level, from children to sturdy old folks, are there. Trying not to be distracted, you crash on through the first half of your course. Ah, an easy leg next: down from the wooded hillside, across the ditch, now work your way around the rice fields, careful not to upset the farmer's crops. Oh! Watch out for cars as you cross the road - but there's no danger here, because the meet organizers have stationed a lookout person to make sure cars don't interrupt the race. Reaching the far side, you navigate between houses and orchards to begin the climb up through densely-wooded bamboo groves. You curse your chosen route, but hope it gains some time. Almost to the control now, but oh! take care not to step into the little Buddhist shrine cemetery. It is one of many which dot the hillsides, symbolized on your map by little black crosses. Finally, you reach the control in the depression, punch in, and are on your way. Before you reach the finish, you will have passed through a variety of interesting terrain, from construction sites to farm-terraced hillsides, to deep and mysterious bamboo forests.

The finish scene is familiar the world over, with small groups gathering over their maps to talk about the day's courses and route choices. What is perhaps unique to Japan are the picnics and sharing of food, which make this a well-rounded social event, while waiting for the prompt posting of times and awards announcements. The train ride home will be several hours long, so the hot soup offered by the organizers, along with the sushi and oranges you've brought, revive your energies.
Waving "Sayonara" to your orienteering friends, you return to the city, refreshed with the day's memories of the natural beauty that Japan's people and countryside have to offer.
Mo ichi do orienteering o suru tsumori desu!
(My interpreter says this means "we will go orienteering again" - Ed.)

We are introducing a new fee category at our meets. From now on, families who have family memberships in the club will pay full fee only for the first two members. Additional family members will pay only $1.00 per registration. We hope this will encourage more families to participate in orienteering. This special fee does not apply to organizations (such as Scouts) with family memberships.

At our last general meeting, Mike Schuh (nominated from the floor) was elected President of COC, but Ardis Dull, who was going to run for Secretary, withdrew because of illness. So, we now have two elected officers, Mike Schuh and Doug Sprugel, who was elected Treasurer last November. We still need a Vice President and a Secretary. On March 9 at the Green Lake Library (7364 E. Green Lake Dr. N.), we will try again to elect a full slate of officers.
The elections will be preceded by a showing of the videotape "Orienteering - A Sense of Direction," which is about the 1985 World O' Championships in Australia.

In brief, our main sources of ordinary (non-Trollhaugen) income are memberships ($809 total) and meet registrations ($983.60: don't ask me where the 60 cents came from). Membership income roughly covers the cost of printing the newsletter, USOF fees, publicity, and administration, while meet fees and map sales nearly cover the cost of putting on meets plus the cost of making and printing maps to hold future meets on. The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League is pretty much self-sustaining; we accidentally made a profit this year, but it isn't planned that way.

The Trollhaugen meet was by far our biggest budget item, both in income and outgo. Based on 1986 figures alone, Trollhaugen almost exactly broke even. This is a little misleading because we spent about $850 in 1985 on the early stages of the mapmaking process, but on the other hand we have about 700 copies of the map left, with a street value of about $875 ($1.25 each). In addition, the equipment bought for the meet (for example, the timing clock) will be useful for many years. In fact, it's debatable whether the equipment should even be considered a Trollhaugen expense. The most important benefits of the Big Meet, of course, are intangible: we have an exciting new place to hold meets (which we will use at least twice this year), generated favorable publicity for Northwestern orienteering, and I for one had a lot of fun working on and running in a major meet without having to fly halfway across the country.

Robert and Sandy Miller's new car (which I told you about last issue) was parked in front of their house when a collision involving two other vehicles threw one of the vehicles against their new car with such force that it couldn't be driven. It has been over two weeks now that they haven't had use of their new toy. Let's hope the body shop makes it as good as new and quickly... For those of you who want to contact Don Denbo, he is at 220 Central St., Stoneham, MA 02180... Club founder Len Englund was discussing having an anniversary celebration of some kind to commemorate COC being in existence for 10 years. Anyone interested in a banquet? Contact Len or a board member with any suggestions or good ideas on it... Anyone who wants to write to Eileen Williams in Japan can contact her at 3-22-8 Jujonakahara, Kita-Ku, Tokyo 114 JAPAN. Use air mail, because surface mail took over two months. Anyone who wants to read her first card, just ask me... We have six new memberships in the club. We need more, so everyone try to enlist a new member or so... If more people tell me what is going on, I will be able to write it up in this column.

Several local residents have been recognized by U.S. Orienteering Team coach Peter Gagarin for their performances in "A" meets in 1986. Heading the list is Nathan Tharp (Nisqually Orienteers), who was one of nine people named to the U.S. Junior Men's A Team. Mary Forbes (Nisqually) was named to the Senior Women's B Team, and Ron Pontius (Nisqually and Blue Star Komplex) was named to the Senior Men's B Team. Named to the "Bullpen" (people who have done well) were Sammamish O.C.'s Dave Enger in M21A, and Cascade's own Debbie Newell and Eileen Williams in F21A, and Jessica Hirschfelder in the Junior Women's division. Debbie was also named "rookie of the year" in class F21A. Congratulations to all these people, and keep up the good work.

One of the many things I want to do as club President this year is to improve communications within the club, especially between the club's executive board and the general membership. One part of this will be this column; others will include regular club meetings and "Pot-0'-Luck" dinners, club picnics, and social get-togethers after our meets.

We will have a club meeting at 6:30 p.m., on March 9 at the Green Lake Library (7464 E. Green Lake Dr. N.). The meeting will start with the showing of the video tape "Orienteering - A Sense of Direction", which features the 1985 World Orienteering Championships in Australia. Afterwards we will have a...
brief business meeting, including the election of club Secretary (and perhaps club Vice President). I will also entertain a BRIEF discussion of club goals and members' concerns.

Plans for the May meeting include a talk on training tips and ideas on how to improve your navigational skills. The July meeting will feature a report from this year's USOF convention and Western U.S. Orienteering Championships, both of which take place in San Diego in June.

The first of this year's Pot-O'-Lucks will be held April 4th; see the details elsewhere in this issue. Judy Cohen, the club's "Social Director," is responsible for organizing these (one will be held every other month), and the other social activities. Call her if you'd like to host one of the Pot-O'-Lucks.

1987 marks the tenth anniversary of Cascade Orienteering Club as an organized club; we will be celebrating this occasion with appropriate festivities this fall. More on this as plans develop - if you'd like to help, give me a call. And next year, in July, we plan to host the 1988 Western U.S. Orienteering Championships. Exciting things are happening - join in the fun and help make them even more exciting!

Knut (Ka-noot) Olsen, 62 year old U.S. champion and Swede extraordinaire, has accepted the publicity coordinator position. I, ex-publicity man, would have liked to be the first to congratulate him, unfortunately that opportunity vanished like a valuable object stolen in the night (the thief, I believe, was the misplacement of "objectives," whether personal or business, before "people"). I was far from first, in seemingly clandestine changes, to learn that someone's dissatisfaction or striving for improvements has, rather than merely expressing itself, taken action. I feel like I just met Rambo.
Such changes should not be so sudden. The Club should maybe not copy the world that the news media brings us: Iranscam, Ivan Boesky in Wall Street, etc.

What I have learned is that doing long stints of volunteer work it is easy to accumulate expectations of return other than enjoying the sport itself. I've learned that the appreciation was there, but like a hard-to-find control feature, I sometimes expected it to look different. Sometimes I asked for it, subtly, like I was asking for money.

PUBLICITY is a big word. It is like saying "I work at Boeing." What Knut does will be truly Knut-Publicity. I will pass along to him tips and tats of info and leads that many, many of you club members have passed to me. (Thanks). Anyone with ideas could help Knut by passing along a name and number or a publication address with your tip.
I would like to thank those who responded to newsletter notices and helped me out. I hope you do the same for Knut, his accent is the only complex thing about Him!

Pleasant weather greeted the more than 80 orienteers that attended the Fifth Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Championships February 14 in Seattle's Seward Park. In addition to the league course, there were four other courses available, ranging from 2 km to over 5 km.

The WIOL course saw some good competition, with many places being decided by just a few seconds. Jeff Coker, from Wilson, was the men's winner, and Pam Kosacki, from Yelm, was the women's winner. In a rare occurence, Nathan Tharp, from Timberline, mispunched near the end of the course, and was disqualified. Tacoma's Wilson High School, with Carl Moore coaching, won the team trophy again.

On the longest course, Dale Width showed that the residents of the wet side of the mountains do not have a monopoly on orienteering prowess. Likewise, Arne Skog and Dave Lilly demonstrated that Sammamish OC has a few good orienteers as well. It is interesting to note that Robert Miller, just 37 seconds slower than Dave, walked/jogged the course due to injuries. Sometimes accuracy can be just as useful as speed...
Debbie Newell and Sandy Kish Miller lead the way on course 3, showing what a bit of experience can do.
A couple of people forgot to punch the last control: Fred Veler, on Course 4, and Don Atkinson on Course 3. Otherwise, they would have had fast times.

After the event, there was a picnic to benefit the school league. This was well received and many commented that they would like to see such offerings become a standard part of orienteering meets in the future.

Thanks go to Kurt Blakstad for design of the novice course and helping set up and take down the finish area; Gary McCausland for help with WIOL registration, setting up, and making the school league awards; Pat Albright for marking the WIOL maps and registration and providing transportation; Dick Diesner for manning the start; Dave Enger for vetting the WIOL course and working at the finish; and Peggy Tharp for organizing the picnic. Due to the large turnout, several club members stepped in without being asked and helped out. These included Debbie Newell and Harvey Friedman, who worked at registration; Arnold Kay, who instructed beginners and then worked with Dave at the finish for the rest of the day; Peggy Tharp, who greeted newcomers; and Carl Moore and Mike Tharp, who assisted at the...

finish. The joy of every meet director, the following volunteered to pick up control markers after the meet: Debbie Newell, Dave Tallent, Pat Albright, and Steve Herber. Arnold Kay also helped pack up afterwards. Mike Schuh set the other four courses and was meet director. Thanks everyone!

At a meeting held on February 7th, a new orienteering organization was born: the Washington State Orienteering Association. Loosely composed of a group of interested orienteers in Washington, the purpose of the association is to help clubs promote and develop orienteering on a statewide basis.

One of the group's priorities is to have a logo (you can't have stationery without a logo), so they are sponsoring a logo design contest. There won't be any prizes (like a round trip ticket to Sweden), but it should be fun.

If you are interested in submitting a logo design, please bring it to the next meeting of the WSOA, which will be in Cle Elum on May 23rd, right after the Kittitas Valley Orienteering Festival (see schedule).
COC's first pot-O-luck of 1987 will be held on April 4th at the home of Judy Cohen, beginning at 6:30 p.m. Bring your favorite dish and see what everyone looks like without mud all over them.
